examples.title: Algorithm: Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
examples.description: Demonstrates how the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions in a cycle
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Fetch_Decode_Execute
INPUT: Program instructions stored in memory
OUTPUT: Executed instruction results

// CPU Registers
REGISTERS:
 PC // Program Counter - holds address of next instruction
 IR // Instruction Register - holds current instruction
 MAR // Memory Address Register - holds address to access
 MBR // Memory Data Register - holds data from/to memory
 ACC // Accumulator - holds arithmetic/logic results

BEGIN
 INITIALIZE: PC = 0, ACC = 0
 
 WHILE (program_running) DO
 // ===== FETCH PHASE =====
 MAR = PC // Load address of next instruction
 READ_MEMORY(MAR) // Get instruction from memory
 IR = MBR // Store instruction in IR
 PC = PC + 1 // Increment PC to next instruction
 
 // ===== DECODE PHASE =====
 OPCODE = EXTRACT_OPCODE(IR) // Get operation code
 OPERAND = EXTRACT_OPERAND(IR) // Get operand value/address
 
 // ===== EXECUTE PHASE =====
 SWITCH (OPCODE) OF
 CASE LOAD:
 MAR = OPERAND
 READ_MEMORY(MAR)
 ACC = MBR
 
 CASE STORE:
 MAR = OPERAND
 MBR = ACC
 WRITE_MEMORY(MAR)
 
 CASE ADD:
 MAR = OPERAND
 READ_MEMORY(MAR)
 ACC = ACC + MBR
 
 CASE SUBTRACT:
 MAR = OPERAND
 READ_MEMORY(MAR)
 ACC = ACC - MBR
 
 CASE HALT:
 program_running = FALSE
 
 END_SWITCH
 
 END_WHILE
 
 RETURN ACC // Return final accumulator value
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental fetch-decode-execute cycle used by all CPUs. The cycle has three main phases: 1) FETCH - retrieves instruction from memory using PC, 2) DECODE - interprets the instruction by extracting opcode and operand, 3) EXECUTE - performs the operation (LOAD, STORE, ADD, etc.). The CPU uses registers (PC, IR, MAR, MBR, ACC) as fast internal storage to manage this process.

examples.title: Python: CPU Simulation with Memory
examples.description: Implements a simple CPU simulation demonstrating fetch-decode-execute cycle and memory operations
examples.code:
"""
CPU Simulation - Demonstrates Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
This simulation models a basic CPU with registers, memory, and instruction execution.
"""

class CPUSimulation:
 def __init__(self, memory_size=256):
 # Initialize CPU registers
 self.PC = 0 # Program Counter
 self.IR = 0 # Instruction Register
 self.ACC = 0 # Accumulator
 self.MAR = 0 # Memory Address Register
 self.MBR = 0 # Memory Data Register
 self.running = True
 
 # Initialize memory (RAM simulation)
 self.memory = [0] * memory_size
 
 def load_program(self, program):
 """Load a program into memory"""
 for i, instruction in enumerate(program):
 self.memory[i] = instruction
 
 def read_memory(self, address):
 """Read data from memory"""
 self.MAR = address
 self.MBR = self.memory[address]
 return self.MBR
 
 def write_memory(self, address, value):
 """Write data to memory"""
 self.MAR = address
 self.memory[address] = value
 self.MBR = value
 
 def fetch(self):
 """Fetch phase - get instruction from memory"""
 self.IR = self.read_memory(self.PC)
 self.PC += 1
 
 def decode(self):
 """Decode phase - extract opcode and operand"""
 # Instruction format: [opcode (upper 4 bits) | operand (lower 4 bits)]
 opcode = (self.IR >> 4) & 0x0F
 operand = self.IR & 0x0F
 return opcode, operand
 
 def execute(self, opcode, operand):
 """Execute phase - perform the operation"""
 opcodes = {
 0x0: ('LOAD', self.op_load),
 0x1: ('STORE', self.op_store),
 0x2: ('ADD', self.op_add),
 0x3: ('SUB', self.op_sub),
 0x4: ('JUMP', self.op_jump),
 0xF: ('HALT', self.op_halt)
 }
 
 if opcode in opcodes:
 name, operation = opcodes[opcode]
 operation(operand)
 return name
 return 'UNKNOWN'
 
 def op_load(self, address):
 """LOAD: Load value from memory address to accumulator"""
 value = self.read_memory(address)
 self.ACC = value
 
 def op_store(self, address):
 """STORE: Store accumulator value to memory address"""
 self.write_memory(address, self.ACC)
 
 def op_add(self, address):
 """ADD: Add value at address to accumulator"""
 value = self.read_memory(address)
 self.ACC += value
 
 def op_sub(self, address):
 """SUB: Subtract value at address from accumulator"""
 value = self.read_memory(address)
 self.ACC -= value
 
 def op_jump(self, address):
 """JUMP: Set PC to address (unconditional jump)"""
 self.PC = address
 
 def op_halt(self, _):
 """HALT: Stop CPU execution"""
 self.running = False
 
 def run(self):
 """Run the CPU cycle continuously"""
 print("=" * 50)
 print("CPU Simulation Started")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 cycle_count = 0
 while self.running:
 cycle_count += 1
 print(f"\n--- Cycle {cycle_count} ---")
 print(f"PC: {self.PC}, ACC: {self.ACC}, IR: {self.IR}")
 
 # Fetch
 self.fetch()
 print(f"FETCH: Loaded instruction {self.IR} from address {self.PC - 1}")
 
 # Decode
 opcode, operand = self.decode()
 print(f"DECODE: Opcode={hex(opcode)}, Operand={operand}")
 
 # Execute
 op_name = self.execute(opcode, operand)
 print(f"EXECUTE: {op_name} (operand={operand})")
 print(f"ACC after execution: {self.ACC}")
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print(f"CPU Stopped after {cycle_count} cycles")
 print(f"Final Accumulator Value: {self.ACC}")
 print("=" * 50)


# Demo: Load a simple program
# Program: Load 5, Add 3, Store result at address 10, Halt
# Instructions: [LOAD from addr 5, ADD addr 3, STORE to addr 10, HALT]
# Memory setup: addr 5 = 10, addr 3 = 5, addr 10 = 0

if __name__ == "__main__":
 cpu = CPUSimulation()
 
 # Set up data in memory
 cpu.memory[5] = 10 # First number
 cpu.memory[3] = 5 # Second number
 cpu.memory[10] = 0 # Result location
 
 # Program: LOAD 5 | ADD 3 | STORE 10 | HALT
 # Instruction format: [opcode | address]
 # LOAD=0x0, ADD=0x2, STORE=0x1, HALT=0xF
 program = [
 0x05, # LOAD from address 5
 0x23, # ADD address 3
 0x1A, # STORE to address 10
 0xF0 # HALT
 ]
 
 cpu.load_program(program)
 cpu.run()
 
 # Display results
 print(f"\nMemory[10] (stored result): {cpu.memory[10]}")
 print(f"Calculation: 10 + 5 = {cpu.ACC}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation simulates a basic CPU with registers and memory. It demonstrates the complete fetch-decode-execute cycle. The CPU uses an instruction format where the upper 4 bits are the opcode and lower 4 bits are the memory address. The simulation includes LOAD, STORE, ADD, SUB, JUMP, and HALT operations. This helps students understand how the CPU actually processes instructions one by one from memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of instructions executed
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is the memory size

examples.title: C: Interrupt Handling Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates interrupt-driven I/O and interrupt service routine handling in C
examples.explanation: This C program simulates interrupt handling in a computer system. It demonstrates: 1) CPU state management with registers (PC, ACC), 2) Interrupt enable/disable flags, 3) I/O device interrupt requests, 4) Interrupt vectors (0x20 for timer, 0x21 for keyboard), 5) Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs), 6) Saving/restoring CPU state during interrupts. The simulation shows how the CPU suspends main program execution to handle urgent events, then returns to normal operation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n CPU cycles, interrupt handling is O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for fixed number of structures

examples.title: Python: Memory Hierarchy Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates the memory hierarchy with caching and access time simulation
examples.code:
"""
Memory Hierarchy Simulation
Demonstrates registers, cache, RAM, and secondary storage with timing.
"""

import time
import random

class MemoryHierarchy:
 """Simulates computer memory hierarchy"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 # Memory characteristics (simulated)
 self.registers = {} # Registers (4 registers)
 self.cache = {} # Cache memory (8 lines)
 self.cache_size = 8
 self.cache_hits = 0
 self.cache_misses = 0
 
 self.ram = {} # Main memory (256 locations)
 self.ram_size = 256
 
 self.disk = {} # Secondary storage (simulated disk)
 self.disk_size = 10000
 
 # Initialize sample data
 self._initialize_memory()
 
 # Access times (in simulated cycles)
 self.REGISTER_TIME = 1
 self.CACHE_TIME = 10
 self.RAM_TIME = 100
 self.DISK_TIME = 1000000
 
 def _initialize_memory(self):
 """Initialize memory with sample data"""
 # Initialize registers
 for i in range(4):
 self.registers[f'R{i}'] = 0
 
 # Initialize RAM with random data
 for addr in range(self.ram_size):
 self.ram[addr] = random.randint(0, 255)
 
 # Initialize disk with sample files
 for addr in range(self.disk_size):
 self.disk[addr] = random.randint(0, 255)
 
 def access_register(self, reg_name):
 """Access data from register - fastest"""
 time.sleep(self.REGISTER_TIME * 0.0001)
 return self.registers.get(reg_name, 0)
 
 def access_cache(self, address):
 """Access data from cache"""
 # Check if data is in cache
 if address in self.cache:
 self.cache_hits += 1
 time.sleep(self.CACHE_TIME * 0.0001)
 return self.cache[address]
 else:
 self.cache_misses += 1
 # Cache miss - load from RAM
 data = self.access_ram(address)
 # Store in cache (simplified - no eviction for demo)
 if len(self.cache) < self.cache_size:
 self.cache[address] = data
 return data
 
 def access_ram(self, address):
 """Access data from main memory (RAM)"""
 time.sleep(self.RAM_TIME * 0.0001)
 return self.ram.get(address, 0)
 
 def access_disk(self, address):
 """Access data from secondary storage (Disk)"""
 time.sleep(self.DISK_TIME * 0.0001)
 return self.disk.get(address, 0)
 
 def read(self, address, location="RAM"):
 """Read from specified memory level"""
 print(f"\n--- Reading from {location} at address {address} ---")
 
 if location == "REGISTER":
 start = time.time()
 data = self.access_register(address)
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 print(f"Data: {data}, Time: ~{self.REGISTER_TIME} cycles")
 return data
 
 elif location == "CACHE":
 start = time.time()
 data = self.access_cache(address)
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 # Determine if hit or miss
 if address in self.cache:
 print(f"Cache HIT! Data: {data}")
 else:
 print(f"Cache MISS! Loaded from RAM. Data: {data}")
 return data
 
 elif location == "RAM":
 start = time.time()
 data = self.access_ram(address)
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 print(f"Data: {data}, Time: ~{self.RAM_TIME} cycles")
 return data
 
 elif location == "DISK":
 start = time.time()
 data = self.access_disk(address)
 elapsed = time.time() - start
 print(f"Data: {data}, Time: ~{self.DISK_TIME} cycles")
 return data
 
 def write_register(self, reg_name, value):
 """Write to register"""
 self.registers[reg_name] = value
 print(f"Wrote {value} to register {reg_name}")
 
 def show_stats(self):
 """Display cache statistics"""
 total = self.cache_hits + self.cache_misses
 hit_rate = (self.cache_hits / total * 100) if total > 0 else 0
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("MEMORY HIERARCHY STATISTICS")
 print("=" * 50)
 print(f"Cache Hits: {self.cache_hits}")
 print(f"Cache Misses: {self.cache_misses}")
 print(f"Hit Rate: {hit_rate:.1f}%")
 print("\nRelative Access Times:")
 print(f" Register: {self.REGISTER_TIME} cycle")
 print(f" Cache: {self.CACHE_TIME} cycles")
 print(f" RAM: {self.RAM_TIME} cycles")
 print(f" Disk: {self.DISK_TIME} cycles")
 print("=" * 50)


def demonstrate_memory_hierarchy():
 """Demonstrate different memory levels"""
 memory = MemoryHierarchy()
 
 print("=" * 50)
 print("MEMORY HIERARCHY DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 # Access register (fastest)
 print("\n1. REGISTER ACCESS (Fastest)")
 memory.write_register('R0', 42)
 val = memory.access_register('R0')
 print(f" Value from R0: {val}")
 
 # First cache access (will be miss)
 print("\n2. CACHE ACCESS (First time - Cache Miss)")
 memory.read(100, "CACHE")
 
 # Second access to same address (should be hit)
 print("\n3. CACHE ACCESS (Second time - Cache Hit)")
 memory.read(100, "CACHE")
 
 # RAM access
 print("\n4. RAM ACCESS (Main Memory)")
 memory.read(100, "RAM")
 
 # Disk access (slowest)
 print("\n5. DISK ACCESS (Slowest - Secondary Storage)")
 memory.read(5000, "DISK")
 
 # More cache operations to show locality
 print("\n6. Demonstrating Spatial Locality")
 for addr in range(100, 108): # Access consecutive addresses
 memory.read(addr, "CACHE")
 
 # Show final statistics
 memory.show_stats()
 
 print("\nKEY INSIGHTS:")
 print("- Registers: Few cycles, but only 4 available")
 print("- Cache: Much faster than RAM when data is cached")
 print("- RAM: Main working memory")
 print("- Disk: Very slow, used for permanent storage")
 print("\nCache works because of:")
 print("- Temporal Locality: Recently accessed items likely accessed again")
 print("- Spatial Locality: Items near each other tend to be accessed together")


if __name__ == "__main__":
 demonstrate_memory_hierarchy()
examples.explanation: This Python program simulates the memory hierarchy showing how data moves between registers, cache, RAM, and disk. It demonstrates temporal locality (re-accessing same data) and spatial locality (accessing adjacent data). The simulation includes realistic timing ratios between memory levels, showing why caching is critical for performance. Students can see cache hits vs misses and understand why frequently accessed data should be kept in faster memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for each memory access, cache lookup is O(1) in this simulation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total memory locations across all levels
